title: UTI — Complicated
oneLiner: Upper or lower UTI with fever, structural/functional abnormality, male sex, pregnancy, immunosuppression, catheter, or recent instrumentation — risk for urosepsis and renal abscess.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Urosepsis:** hypotension (MAP <65), lactate >2, altered mental state — needs immediate IV fluids, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and senior review.
- **Acute kidney injury:** oliguria <0.5 mL/kg/h for >6h, rising creatinine — rule out obstructive uropathy (e.g. stone, stricture) with bedside bladder scan + urgent renal ultrasound.
- **Renal abscess / emphysematous pyelonephritis:** persistent high fever despite antipyretics, extreme flank tenderness, or crepitus over flank — CT KUB first; needs drainage and/or urology/surgery input.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** dysuria, frequency, suprapubic pain, flank pain (unilateral or bilateral), fevers/rigors, nausea/vomiting, new confusion (elderly).
- **Key exam findings:** fever >38°C, tachycardia, costovertebral angle tenderness (unilateral), suprapubic tenderness; hypotension, altered GCS if septic.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Pyelonephritis:** flank pain + fever + CVA tenderness.
  - **Lower UTI (cystitis):** suprapubic pain, dysuria, frequency; no systemic signs.
  - **Urolithiasis:** colicky flank pain radiating to groin, haematuria, no fever unless infected.
  - **Acute prostatitis (men):** perineal pain, fever, boggy/tender prostate on DRE, difficulty voiding.
  - **Intra-abdominal sepsis (e.g. diverticulitis, appendicitis):** abdominal rebound/guarding, bowel changes, different pain pattern.
- **Splitter:** flank pain + fever = pyelonephritis; suprapubic pain + no fever = cystitis.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Urine dipstick: nitrites, leukocyte esterase, blood, protein.
  - Urinalysis with microscopy: WBC >10/hpf, bacteria.
  - Bedside bladder scan (post-void residual if urinary retention suspected).
  - Pregnancy test in all women of childbearing age.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (raised WCC, neutrophils), CRP, U&E, creatinine, lactate.
  - Blood cultures (two sets) before antibiotics if febrile/septic.
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Renal ultrasound** — exclude obstruction (hydronephrosis), abscess, stones.
  - Second-line: **CT KUB** (non-contrast) if suspicion of stones/abscess/emphysematous changes; CT with contrast if perinephric collection suspected.
- **Special tests:**
  - Urine culture + sensitivities (mid-stream or catheter specimen) — always before antibiotics if possible.
  - In men: consider DRE + prostate-specific antigen (PSA) if prostatitis suspected (but avoid massage if acute).
- **Diagnostic criteria (SEPSIS-3):** qSOFA ≥2 (RR ≥22, SBP ≤100, altered GCS) + source = UTI → consider Sepsis 6.
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood cultures** before antibiotics in any patient with fever or systemic symptoms. Also: **pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age (rule out gestation and guide antibiotic choice).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Sepsis 6** if septic: take blood cultures, measure lactate, start IV fluids (crystalloid 500 mL bolus, reassess), give broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour (see Drugs), start monitoring (hourly urine output, NEWS2), escalate to senior.
  - If obstructed (hydronephrosis on USS): urgent urology referral for decompression (nephrostomy or stent).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics (guided by cultures when available).
  - Remove or replace any indwelling catheter >7 days; send catheter specimen.
  - Monitor urine output (target >0.5 mL/kg/h), renal function daily.
  - If no improvement at 48h: repeat imaging (CT KUB) to look for abscess/obstruction.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Switch to oral antibiotics once afebrile >24-48h and oral tolerance confirmed (sensitivity-guided).
  - Duration: 7–14 days for complicated pyelonephritis; 14–21 days for prostatitis; 3–7 days for catheter-associated UTI after catheter removal.
  - Consider six-week antibiotic course for suspected renal abscess or emphysematous pyelonephritis.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or Hartmann’s at 80–100 mL/h if normal renal function; adjust for AKI (caution with potassium).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated.
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet; NBM only if procedure planned (e.g. stent insertion).
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 q1h initially until stable, then q4h; strict fluid balance chart.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (IV, empirical, local policy dependent):**
  - **Gentamicin:** 7 mg/kg IV once daily (single dose) [check local policy for dosing, e.g. 5–7 mg/kg based on ideal body weight] — check baseline U&E, levels before second dose (trough <1 mg/L).
  - **Piperacillin-tazobactam (Tazocin):** 4.5 g IV TDS (if high risk of resistance or severe sepsis).
  - **Ceftriaxone:** 2 g IV OD (for mild-moderate complicated UTI, not ceftriaxone if pseudomonas suspected).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Ciprofloxacin:** 500 mg PO/IV BD (if susceptible, but avoid as monotherapy for severe sepsis due to resistance).
  - **Cefuroxime:** 750 mg IV TDS (if mild).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Gentamicin: avoid with other nephrotoxins (e.g. NSAIDs, ACEi, IV contrast); use caution in pre-existing AKI.
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: adjust dose if CrCl <20 mL/min; avoid in penicillin allergy.
  - Ciprofloxacin: avoid in pregnancy, epilepsy, tendinopathy; QT prolongation risk.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gentamicin: dose reduction required — use Hartford nomogram or [check local policy].
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: reduce to 4.5 g IV BD if CrCl 20–40 mL/min; 4.5 g IV daily if <20.
  - Ceftriaxone: no adjustment in renal impairment; caution in hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin without monitoring** — failure to check levels before second dose leads to ototoxicity/nephrotoxicity. Always document trough level timing.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Urosepsis / septic shock (high mortality).
  - Acute kidney injury (prerenal or postrenal).
  - Renal abscess, perinephric abscess.
  - Emphysematous pyelonephritis (gas in renal parenchyma) — surgical emergency.
  - Papillary necrosis (haematuria, sloughing papillae → obstruction).
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent infections.
  - Renal scarring → hypertension, chronic kidney disease.
  - Chronic pyelonephritis (in children, reflux nephropathy).
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality of complicated UTI is <5% if treated early; rises to 20–40% if urosepsis develops. Emphysematous pyelonephritis has mortality up to 25% even with aggressive management. Most patients recover fully with source control and appropriate antibiotics.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always send urine culture before starting antibiotics** — even a single dose can mask the organism. Bagged specimen (child) or mid-stream in adults.
- **In catheter-associated UTI, replace the catheter** (or remove it) — biofilm on old catheter perpetuates infection.
- **For men with suspected prostatitis, examine the prostate gently** — a boggy, tender prostate confirms; avoid vigorous massage.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Using urine dipstick as sole diagnostic** — false negatives for leukocyte esterase can occur in complicated UTI; always send culture.
- **Not imaging in patients who fail to improve at 48h** — an obstructed kidney needs urgent drainage.
- **Prescribing ciprofloxacin without checking local resistance** — many areas have high E. coli resistance; use only when susceptibility confirmed.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Fever persists >48h after starting antibiotics, worsening flank pain, vomiting unable to keep meds/oral fluids, confusion, decreased urine output (<500 mL/day).
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 5–7 days with urine culture results; if recurrent, refer to urology for further imaging (CT-IVP, video urodynamics). Women with recurrent simple infection may need prophylactic antibiotics.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially respiratory or ABP criteria).
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L refractory to fluid challenge.
  - Oliguric (<0.5 mL/kg/h) for >6h despite fluids.
  - GCS falling or new confusion.
  - Need for vasopressors (e.g. noradrenaline) — contact ICU for admission.
- **Call urology urgently if:** obstructed kidney (hydronephrosis) on USS, or suspected renal abscess not responding to antibiotics.

rawMarkdown:
## UTI — Complicated
**One-liner:** Upper or lower UTI with fever, structural/functional abnormality, male sex, pregnancy, immunosuppression, catheter, or recent instrumentation — risk for urosepsis and renal abscess.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Urosepsis:** hypotension (MAP <65), lactate >2, altered mental state — needs immediate IV fluids, blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and senior review.
- **Acute kidney injury:** oliguria <0.5 mL/kg/h for >6h, rising creatinine — rule out obstructive uropathy (e.g. stone, stricture) with bedside bladder scan + urgent renal ultrasound.
- **Renal abscess / emphysematous pyelonephritis:** persistent high fever despite antipyretics, extreme flank tenderness, or crepitus over flank — CT KUB first; needs drainage and/or urology/surgery input.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** dysuria, frequency, suprapubic pain, flank pain (unilateral or bilateral), fevers/rigors, nausea/vomiting, new confusion (elderly).
- **Key exam findings:** fever >38°C, tachycardia, costovertebral angle tenderness (unilateral), suprapubic tenderness; hypotension, altered GCS if septic.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Pyelonephritis:** flank pain + fever + CVA tenderness.
  - **Lower UTI (cystitis):** suprapubic pain, dysuria, frequency; no systemic signs.
  - **Urolithiasis:** colicky flank pain radiating to groin, haematuria, no fever unless infected.
  - **Acute prostatitis (men):** perineal pain, fever, boggy/tender prostate on DRE, difficulty voiding.
  - **Intra-abdominal sepsis (e.g. diverticulitis, appendicitis):** abdominal rebound/guarding, bowel changes, different pain pattern.
- **Splitter:** flank pain + fever = pyelonephritis; suprapubic pain + no fever = cystitis.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Urine dipstick: nitrites, leukocyte esterase, blood, protein.
  - Urinalysis with microscopy: WBC >10/hpf, bacteria.
  - Bedside bladder scan (post-void residual if urinary retention suspected).
  - Pregnancy test in all women of childbearing age.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (raised WCC, neutrophils), CRP, U&E, creatinine, lactate.
  - Blood cultures (two sets) before antibiotics if febrile/septic.
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Renal ultrasound** — exclude obstruction (hydronephrosis), abscess, stones.
  - Second-line: **CT KUB** (non-contrast) if suspicion of stones/abscess/emphysematous changes; CT with contrast if perinephric collection suspected.
- **Special tests:**
  - Urine culture + sensitivities (mid-stream or catheter specimen) — always before antibiotics if possible.
  - In men: consider DRE + prostate-specific antigen (PSA) if prostatitis suspected (but avoid massage if acute).
- **Diagnostic criteria (SEPSIS-3):** qSOFA ≥2 (RR ≥22, SBP ≤100, altered GCS) + source = UTI → consider Sepsis 6.
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood cultures** before antibiotics in any patient with fever or systemic symptoms. Also: **pregnancy test** in women of childbearing age (rule out gestation and guide antibiotic choice).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Sepsis 6** if septic: take blood cultures, measure lactate, start IV fluids (crystalloid 500 mL bolus, reassess), give broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour (see Drugs), start monitoring (hourly urine output, NEWS2), escalate to senior.
  - If obstructed (hydronephrosis on USS): urgent urology referral for decompression (nephrostomy or stent).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics (guided by cultures when available).
  - Remove or replace any indwelling catheter >7 days; send catheter specimen.
  - Monitor urine output (target >0.5 mL/kg/h), renal function daily.
  - If no improvement at 48h: repeat imaging (CT KUB) to look for abscess/obstruction.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Switch to oral antibiotics once afebrile >24-48h and oral tolerance confirmed (sensitivity-guided).
  - Duration: 7–14 days for complicated pyelonephritis; 14–21 days for prostatitis; 3–7 days for catheter-associated UTI after catheter removal.
  - Consider six-week antibiotic course for suspected renal abscess or emphysematous pyelonephritis.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or Hartmann’s at 80–100 mL/h if normal renal function; adjust for AKI (caution with potassium).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless contraindicated.
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet; NBM only if procedure planned (e.g. stent insertion).
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 q1h initially until stable, then q4h; strict fluid balance chart.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (IV, empirical, local policy dependent):**
  - **Gentamicin:** 7 mg/kg IV once daily (single dose) [check local policy for dosing, e.g. 5–7 mg/kg based on ideal body weight] — check baseline U&E, levels before second dose (trough <1 mg/L).
  - **Piperacillin-tazobactam (Tazocin):** 4.5 g IV TDS (if high risk of resistance or severe sepsis).
  - **Ceftriaxone:** 2 g IV OD (for mild-moderate complicated UTI, not ceftriaxone if pseudomonas suspected).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Ciprofloxacin:** 500 mg PO/IV BD (if susceptible, but avoid as monotherapy for severe sepsis due to resistance).
  - **Cefuroxime:** 750 mg IV TDS (if mild).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Gentamicin: avoid with other nephrotoxins (e.g. NSAIDs, ACEi, IV contrast); use caution in pre-existing AKI.
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: adjust dose if CrCl <20 mL/min; avoid in penicillin allergy.
  - Ciprofloxacin: avoid in pregnancy, epilepsy, tendinopathy; QT prolongation risk.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Gentamicin: dose reduction required — use Hartford nomogram or [check local policy].
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: reduce to 4.5 g IV BD if CrCl 20–40 mL/min; 4.5 g IV daily if <20.
  - Ceftriaxone: no adjustment in renal impairment; caution in hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin without monitoring** — failure to check levels before second dose leads to ototoxicity/nephrotoxicity. Always document trough level timing.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Urosepsis / septic shock (high mortality).
  - Acute kidney injury (prerenal or postrenal).
  - Renal abscess, perinephric abscess.
  - Emphysematous pyelonephritis (gas in renal parenchyma) — surgical emergency.
  - Papillary necrosis (haematuria, sloughing papillae → obstruction).
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent infections.
  - Renal scarring → hypertension, chronic kidney disease.
  - Chronic pyelonephritis (in children, reflux nephropathy).

### Prognosis
- Mortality of complicated UTI is <5% if treated early; rises to 20–40% if urosepsis develops. Emphysematous pyelonephritis has mortality up to 25% even with aggressive management. Most patients recover fully with source control and appropriate antibiotics.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always send urine culture before starting antibiotics** — even a single dose can mask the organism. Bagged specimen (child) or mid-stream in adults.
- **In catheter-associated UTI, replace the catheter** (or remove it) — biofilm on old catheter perpetuates infection.
- **For men with suspected prostatitis, examine the prostate gently** — a boggy, tender prostate confirms; avoid vigorous massage.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Using urine dipstick as sole diagnostic** — false negatives for leukocyte esterase can occur in complicated UTI; always send culture.
- **Not imaging in patients who fail to improve at 48h** — an obstructed kidney needs urgent drainage.
- **Prescribing ciprofloxacin without checking local resistance** — many areas have high E. coli resistance; use only when susceptibility confirmed.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Fever persists >48h after starting antibiotics, worsening flank pain, vomiting unable to keep meds/oral fluids, confusion, decreased urine output (<500 mL/day).
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 5–7 days with urine culture results; if recurrent, refer to urology for further imaging (CT-IVP, video urodynamics). Women with recurrent simple infection may need prophylactic antibiotics.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially respiratory or ABP criteria).
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L refractory to fluid challenge.
  - Oliguric (<0.5 mL/kg/h) for >6h despite fluids.
  - GCS falling or new confusion.
  - Need for vasopressors (e.g. noradrenaline) — contact ICU for admission.
- **Call urology urgently if:** obstructed kidney (hydronephrosis) on USS, or suspected renal abscess not responding to antibiotics.
